title: Modifier Placement Mistakes - 50 Mistakes
definition: A modifier is a word or phrase that describes something else in the sentence - when it's placed in the wrong spot, it can accidentally describe the wrong thing, creating a confusing or even funny meaning. Here are 50 common modifier mistakes, explained simply, with the fix that puts the modifier next to the word it should describe.

commonMistakes.wrong: Running down the street, the bus was missed by John.
commonMistakes.right: Running down the street, John missed the bus.
commonMistakes.why: This is called a 'dangling modifier' - the phrase 'running down the street' should describe John, but as written, it seems to describe 'the bus', which doesn't make sense (buses don't run down streets on foot). Always place the modifier right next to the person or thing it's actually describing - here, that's John, so he needs to be the very next word.

commonMistakes.wrong: Walking to school, the rain started falling.
commonMistakes.right: While I was walking to school, the rain started falling.
commonMistakes.why: As written, 'walking to school' seems to describe 'the rain', which doesn't make sense - rain doesn't walk. Add a subject who is actually doing the walking, either by rewriting with 'while I was walking' or restructuring the sentence entirely.

commonMistakes.wrong: After finishing the assignment, the teacher collected our papers.
commonMistakes.right: After we finished the assignment, the teacher collected our papers.
commonMistakes.why: This sentence implies the TEACHER finished the assignment, when really it was the students - the modifier is describing the wrong person. Make sure the subject right after the modifier phrase is the one who actually performed that action.

commonMistakes.wrong: Covered in chocolate, I ate the cake.
commonMistakes.right: I ate the cake covered in chocolate.
commonMistakes.why: As written, 'covered in chocolate' seems to describe the person (I), not the cake, which is a strange and unintended image. Move the modifier closer to what it's actually describing - the cake, not the eater.

commonMistakes.wrong: Barking loudly, the mailman was scared by the dog.
commonMistakes.right: Barking loudly, the dog scared the mailman.
commonMistakes.why: The phrase 'barking loudly' should describe the dog, but the sentence structure makes it seem to describe the mailman, which doesn't make sense. Place the true subject of the action (the dog) immediately after the modifier.

commonMistakes.wrong: Flying over the mountains, the view was breathtaking for the passengers.
commonMistakes.right: Flying over the mountains, the passengers saw a breathtaking view.
commonMistakes.why: As written, 'the view' can't be flying - it's the passengers who are flying, so they need to be the subject right after the modifier. Whenever a modifier describes an action a person is doing, make sure that person appears immediately after it.

commonMistakes.wrong: Having finished dinner, the dishes were washed by Sarah.
commonMistakes.right: Having finished dinner, Sarah washed the dishes.
commonMistakes.why: The dishes didn't finish dinner - Sarah did, so she should be the subject following the modifier phrase. This same dangling modifier pattern appears often when sentences are written in passive voice by mistake.

commonMistakes.wrong: Excited about the trip, the bags were packed quickly.
commonMistakes.right: Excited about the trip, she packed the bags quickly.
commonMistakes.why: Bags can't feel excitement - a person can, so the sentence needs a human subject right after the modifier. Always ask: who or what can actually feel or do what the modifier describes?

commonMistakes.wrong: Tired after a long day, the bed felt very comfortable.
commonMistakes.right: Tired after a long day, I found the bed very comfortable.
commonMistakes.why: The bed can't feel tired - only a person can, so the true subject (I, she, he) needs to follow the modifier directly. This is one of the most common dangling modifier patterns, worth watching for carefully.

commonMistakes.wrong: Driving through the city, the traffic was heavy.
commonMistakes.right: Driving through the city, we noticed the traffic was heavy.
commonMistakes.why: Traffic can't drive through a city - a person does, so the sentence needs a human subject immediately following the modifier. Rewrite so the driver (we, I, he) comes right after the introductory phrase.

commonMistakes.wrong: I only ate two slices of the pizza.
commonMistakes.right: I ate only two slices of the pizza.
commonMistakes.why: This is a misplaced modifier - 'only' is currently placed to suggest 'the only thing I did was eat' (rather than share, buy, etc.), when the intended meaning is limiting the NUMBER of slices. Move 'only' directly before the word it's meant to limit - here, 'two slices'.

commonMistakes.wrong: She almost drove her kids to school every day.
commonMistakes.right: She drove her kids to school almost every day.
commonMistakes.why: As written, 'almost drove' suggests she nearly had an accident or nearly didn't drive at all, but the intended meaning is that this happened on ALMOST every day (meaning frequently, but not quite always). Move 'almost' right before 'every day' to fix the intended meaning.

commonMistakes.wrong: I just want to say one thing to you.
commonMistakes.right: I want to say just one thing to you.
commonMistakes.why: Placing 'just' before 'want' suggests a limitation on wanting itself, but the intended meaning is limiting the number of things you want to say (just one). Move 'just' directly before the word it's actually limiting - 'one thing'.

commonMistakes.wrong: He nearly failed all of his classes.
commonMistakes.right: He failed nearly all of his classes.
commonMistakes.why: As written, 'nearly failed' suggests he almost failed but didn't, while the intended meaning is that he failed almost ALL of his classes (meaning most, but not literally every one). Move 'nearly' right before 'all' to express the intended meaning about quantity.

commonMistakes.wrong: The dog only barks at strangers.
commonMistakes.right: The dog barks only at strangers.
commonMistakes.why: As written, 'only barks' could mean barking is the only thing the dog does (compared to other actions), but the intended meaning is that strangers are the only thing it barks AT. Move 'only' right before 'at strangers' to clarify the intended limitation.

commonMistakes.wrong: We nearly watched the entire movie before falling asleep.
commonMistakes.right: We watched nearly the entire movie before falling asleep.
commonMistakes.why: 'Nearly watched' suggests almost starting to watch, but the intended meaning is watching almost the ENTIRE movie (most of it, but not literally all). Move 'nearly' before 'the entire movie' to express the correct meaning about quantity watched.

commonMistakes.wrong: I found a dog walking down the street.
commonMistakes.right: While walking down the street, I found a dog. / I found a dog that was walking down the street.
commonMistakes.why: As written, this sentence is ambiguous - it's unclear whether the SPEAKER or the DOG was walking down the street. Rewrite the sentence to make it completely clear who was actually doing the walking.

commonMistakes.wrong: She served sandwiches to the children on paper plates.
commonMistakes.right: She served sandwiches on paper plates to the children.
commonMistakes.why: As written, it sounds like the children were on the paper plates, when really the sandwiches were served on paper plates. Move the modifier ('on paper plates') closer to the word it's actually describing - sandwiches, not children.

commonMistakes.wrong: I saw a beautiful sunset driving to work.
commonMistakes.right: While driving to work, I saw a beautiful sunset.
commonMistakes.why: As written, this sentence could suggest the sunset was driving to work, which is impossible. Clarify by placing 'while driving to work' at the beginning, clearly connected to 'I'.

commonMistakes.wrong: The waiter brought a plate of pasta to the customer covered in cheese.
commonMistakes.right: The waiter brought the customer a plate of pasta covered in cheese.
commonMistakes.why: As written, it sounds like the CUSTOMER is covered in cheese, when really the pasta is. Move the modifier phrase ('covered in cheese') right after the word it describes - pasta, not customer.

commonMistakes.wrong: I gave my brother a book that I borrowed from the library on his birthday.
commonMistakes.right: On his birthday, I gave my brother a book that I borrowed from the library.
commonMistakes.why: As written, this sentence is confusingly ordered, making it seem like the birthday and the library visit are connected when they aren't clearly separated. Move 'on his birthday' closer to the beginning or right after 'gave my brother' for clarity.

commonMistakes.wrong: I read an article about earthquakes in the newspaper this morning.
commonMistakes.right: This morning, I read an article about earthquakes in the newspaper.
commonMistakes.why: While this sentence isn't strictly incorrect, moving the time phrase 'this morning' to the front makes the sentence flow better and reduces the pile-up of descriptive phrases at the end. Consider spreading out multiple modifiers rather than stacking them all at the end of a sentence.

commonMistakes.wrong: The teacher explained the homework to the students clearly and simply written on the board.
commonMistakes.right: The teacher clearly and simply explained the homework, which was written on the board, to the students.
commonMistakes.why: This sentence crams too many modifiers together, making it unclear what 'written on the board' refers to. Break up overloaded sentences and place each modifier clearly next to what it describes.

commonMistakes.wrong: I almost spent all my savings on a new laptop.
commonMistakes.right: I spent almost all my savings on a new laptop.
commonMistakes.why: 'Almost spent' suggests you nearly made a purchase but didn't, while the intended meaning is spending almost ALL your savings (most, not literally all). Move 'almost' before 'all' to correctly show the amount spent.

commonMistakes.wrong: He only speaks to me when he needs a favor.
commonMistakes.right: He speaks to me only when he needs a favor.
commonMistakes.why: As written, 'only speaks' could mean speaking is the only action he takes (versus writing, calling, etc.), while the intended meaning limits WHEN he speaks to you. Move 'only' directly before 'when' to clarify the timing restriction.

commonMistakes.wrong: The scientist discovered a new species of frog studying the rainforest.
commonMistakes.right: While studying the rainforest, the scientist discovered a new species of frog.
commonMistakes.why: As written, this could confusingly suggest the frog was studying the rainforest. Clarify by placing 'while studying the rainforest' clearly connected to the scientist at the start of the sentence.

commonMistakes.wrong: I bought a shirt for my father with a floral pattern.
commonMistakes.right: I bought a shirt with a floral pattern for my father.
commonMistakes.why: As written, it sounds like the FATHER has a floral pattern, when really the shirt does. Move the modifier ('with a floral pattern') closer to what it's describing - the shirt, not the father.

commonMistakes.wrong: We watched the fireworks with our neighbors from the balcony.
commonMistakes.right: From the balcony, we watched the fireworks with our neighbors.
commonMistakes.why: While not completely wrong, moving 'from the balcony' to the front clarifies that it describes where the whole group was watching from, avoiding any ambiguity about what it modifies. Placing location phrases at the start often improves clarity when a sentence has multiple modifiers.

commonMistakes.wrong: The manager offered a solution to the problem that was practical.
commonMistakes.right: The manager offered a practical solution to the problem.
commonMistakes.why: As written, it's unclear whether 'that was practical' describes the solution or the problem. Move the modifier ('practical') directly before the word it clearly describes - solution.

commonMistakes.wrong: I saw a man walking my dog.
commonMistakes.right: While walking my dog, I saw a man. / I saw a man who was walking a dog.
commonMistakes.why: This sentence is ambiguous - it's unclear whether the SPEAKER or the MAN was walking the dog. Rewrite so it's completely clear who is actually doing the walking.

commonMistakes.wrong: She gave the medicine to her son that tasted terrible.
commonMistakes.right: She gave her son the medicine that tasted terrible.
commonMistakes.why: As written, it sounds like the SON tastes terrible, when really the medicine does. Move the descriptive clause right next to the word it describes - medicine, not son.

commonMistakes.wrong: He nearly ate the whole pizza by himself.
commonMistakes.right: He ate nearly the whole pizza by himself.
commonMistakes.why: 'Nearly ate' suggests he almost started eating but didn't, while the intended meaning is eating almost the WHOLE pizza. Move 'nearly' before 'the whole pizza' to express the correct meaning about quantity eaten.

commonMistakes.wrong: I bought a new phone from the store that has a great camera.
commonMistakes.right: I bought a new phone that has a great camera from the store.
commonMistakes.why: As written, it sounds like the STORE has a great camera, when really the phone does. Move the descriptive clause directly after the word it's meant to describe.

commonMistakes.wrong: The company hired an employee with excellent references quickly.
commonMistakes.right: The company quickly hired an employee with excellent references.
commonMistakes.why: As written, 'quickly' at the end could seem to describe the references rather than the hiring process. Move 'quickly' closer to the verb it's actually describing - hired.

commonMistakes.wrong: I only drink coffee in the morning.
commonMistakes.right: I drink coffee only in the morning.
commonMistakes.why: As written, 'only drink' could suggest drinking is the only thing you do with coffee (versus buying, smelling, etc.), while the intended meaning limits WHEN you drink it. Move 'only' directly before 'in the morning' to clarify the timing.

commonMistakes.wrong: She almost cooked dinner for the entire family.
commonMistakes.right: She cooked dinner for almost the entire family.
commonMistakes.why: 'Almost cooked' suggests she nearly started cooking but didn't, while the intended meaning is cooking for almost the ENTIRE family (most, not all). Move 'almost' before 'the entire family' to show the correct meaning about who was served.

commonMistakes.wrong: I found my keys looking under the couch.
commonMistakes.right: While looking under the couch, I found my keys.
commonMistakes.why: As written, this could suggest the KEYS were looking under the couch, which is impossible. Clarify by placing 'while looking under the couch' clearly connected to 'I' at the start.

commonMistakes.wrong: The doctor gave advice to the patient that was very helpful.
commonMistakes.right: The doctor gave the patient very helpful advice.
commonMistakes.why: As written, it's ambiguous whether 'that was very helpful' describes the advice or the patient. Move the modifier directly next to 'advice', the word it's meant to describe.

commonMistakes.wrong: I only need five minutes to finish this.
commonMistakes.right: I need only five minutes to finish this.
commonMistakes.why: As written, 'only need' could suggest needing is the only thing happening (versus wanting, requesting, etc.), while the intended meaning limits the AMOUNT of time. Move 'only' directly before 'five minutes' to clarify the limitation on quantity.

commonMistakes.wrong: She wrapped a gift for her friend in colorful paper.
commonMistakes.right: She wrapped a gift in colorful paper for her friend.
commonMistakes.why: As written, it sounds like the FRIEND is wrapped in colorful paper, when really the gift is. Move the modifier phrase directly after the word it's meant to describe.

commonMistakes.wrong: He almost finished reading the entire book in one day.
commonMistakes.right: He finished reading almost the entire book in one day.
commonMistakes.why: 'Almost finished' suggests he nearly completed the task but didn't, while the intended meaning is finishing almost the WHOLE book (most, not literally all). Move 'almost' before 'the entire book' for the correct meaning.

commonMistakes.wrong: The store sells clothes for children made of organic cotton.
commonMistakes.right: The store sells clothes made of organic cotton for children.
commonMistakes.why: As written, it sounds like the CHILDREN are made of organic cotton, when really the clothes are. Move the modifier directly after 'clothes', the word it should describe.

commonMistakes.wrong: I gave a present to my sister wrapped in gold paper.
commonMistakes.right: I gave my sister a present wrapped in gold paper.
commonMistakes.why: As written, it sounds like the SISTER is wrapped in gold paper, when really the present is. Rearrange so the modifier sits right next to the present, not the sister.

commonMistakes.wrong: We nearly drove the entire distance without stopping.
commonMistakes.right: We drove nearly the entire distance without stopping.
commonMistakes.why: 'Nearly drove' suggests almost starting the drive but not quite, while the intended meaning is driving almost the WHOLE distance. Move 'nearly' before 'the entire distance' for the correct meaning.

commonMistakes.wrong: The librarian recommended a book to the student that won several awards.
commonMistakes.right: The librarian recommended a book that won several awards to the student.
commonMistakes.why: As written, it's ambiguous whether the book or the student won awards. Move the descriptive clause ('that won several awards') directly next to 'book', the word it should describe.

commonMistakes.wrong: I only saw her once at the conference.
commonMistakes.right: I saw her only once at the conference.
commonMistakes.why: As written, 'only saw' could suggest seeing is the only thing that happened (versus talking, meeting, etc.), while the intended meaning limits the NUMBER of times. Move 'only' directly before 'once' to clarify the correct limitation.

commonMistakes.wrong: The chef prepared a meal for the guests using fresh ingredients.
commonMistakes.right: Using fresh ingredients, the chef prepared a meal for the guests.
commonMistakes.why: While technically understandable, moving 'using fresh ingredients' to the front makes it immediately clear that it describes the chef's method, not something at the end that could be misread as describing the guests. Placing method-related modifiers near the subject often improves clarity.

commonMistakes.wrong: She almost spoke to every person at the party.
commonMistakes.right: She spoke to almost every person at the party.
commonMistakes.why: 'Almost spoke' suggests she nearly began speaking but didn't, while the intended meaning is speaking to almost EVERY person (most, but not literally all). Move 'almost' before 'every person' for the correct meaning about quantity.

commonMistakes.wrong: I only bought this for you, not for anyone else.
commonMistakes.right: I bought this only for you, not for anyone else.
commonMistakes.why: As written, 'only bought' could suggest buying is the only action taken, while the intended meaning limits WHO it was bought for. Move 'only' directly before 'for you' to clarify the intended limitation.

commonMistakes.wrong: The company delivered a package to the customer damaged in shipping.
commonMistakes.right: The company delivered a package damaged in shipping to the customer.
commonMistakes.why: As written, it sounds like the CUSTOMER is damaged, when really the package is. Move the modifier phrase ('damaged in shipping') directly next to 'package', the word it's meant to describe.

quiz.question: Which sentence correctly places the modifier?
options: Running down the street, the bus was missed by John.
Running down the street, John missed the bus.
The bus, running down the street, was missed by John.
John was missed by the bus running down the street.
quiz.answer: Running down the street, John missed the bus.
quiz.explanation: The modifier 'running down the street' should describe John, who must appear right after it.

quiz.sentence: I only ate two slices of the pizza.
quiz.correction: I ate only two slices of the pizza.
quiz.explanation: 'Only' should be placed directly before the word it limits - here, 'two slices'.

quiz.question: Which sentence correctly describes a dog barking, not a mailman?
options: Barking loudly, the mailman was scared by the dog.
Barking loudly, the dog scared the mailman.
The mailman, barking loudly, scared the dog.
The dog was scared, barking loudly, by the mailman.
quiz.answer: Barking loudly, the dog scared the mailman.
quiz.explanation: The dog is the one barking, so it must be the subject right after the modifier.

quiz.question: He failed ___ all of his classes. (correct placement of 'nearly')
quiz.explanation: 'Nearly' should go right before 'all', showing that almost all (not literally all) classes were failed.

quiz.question: What is a 'dangling modifier'?
options: A modifier describing the wrong thing because of its placement
quiz.answer: A modifier describing the wrong thing because of its placement
quiz.explanation: Dangling modifiers create confusion by seeming to describe something other than what was intended.
